terms.definition: A single musical sound with a specific pitch (e.g., C, D, E).
How high or low a note sounds, determined by the frequency of vibration.
The interval between one musical pitch and another with double its frequency; they share the same note name.
A sequence of musical notes ordered by pitch, spanning an octave.
A group of notes centered around a root note (tonic) that forms the harmonic foundation of a piece.
The distance in pitch between two musical notes.
A symbol indicating a note is raised by one half-step (one fret).
A symbol indicating a note is lowered by one half-step (one fret).
The speed at which a piece of music is played, usually measured in Beats Per Minute (BPM).
A notation indicating how many beats are in a measure and which note value receives one beat (e.g., 4/4).
A small segment of music contained between two vertical lines on a staff, representing a specific number of beats.
A group of notes played together, typically at least three.
The most basic chord type, consisting of a root, a third, and a fifth.
A chord with a bright sound built from a root, major third, and perfect fifth.
A chord with a darker sound built from a root, minor third, and perfect fifth.
A tense chord built from a root, minor third, and flattened fifth.
A chord with a raised fifth, creating an ethereal, unresolved sound.
A triad with an added fourth note, an interval of a seventh above the root (e.g., Cmaj7, G7).
A two-note chord consisting of the root and the fifth, common in rock and metal.
A 'broken' chord where the notes are played individually in sequence rather than simultaneously.
A chord where a note other than the root is the lowest sounding note (the bass).
A sequence of chords played in order to create a harmonic movement.
A system used to analyze chord functions within a key (e.g., I-IV-V).
The specific arrangement of the notes within a chord.
Core components made of steel or nylon that vibrate to produce sound.
A metal strip on the neck that shortens the vibrating length of a string to change pitch.
The wooden surface on the front of the neck where the player presses the strings.
The piece of material at the end of the fretboard that supports the strings near the headstock.
The part of the guitar that anchors the strings to the body and transmits vibrations.
The part at the top of the neck that holds the tuning pegs.
Geared mechanisms used to tighten or loosen strings to adjust their pitch.
Magnets wrapped in wire that convert string vibrations into electrical signals (Electric Guitar).
The height of the strings above the fretboard.
The accuracy of a guitar's pitch across the entire length of the fretboard.
An adjustable metal rod inside the neck used to control its curvature.
A rhythmic movement of the hand or pick across multiple strings.
A technique using strictly alternating downward and upward pick strokes.
Sounding a note by sharply bringing a finger down onto the fretboard.
Sounding a lower note by 'plucking' the string with a fretting finger as you release it.
Moving from one fret to another while maintaining pressure on the string.
Physically pushing the string across the fret to increase tension and raise the pitch.
Rapidly and slightly varying the pitch of a note to add expression.
Resting the side of the picking hand across the strings near the bridge to produce a muffled, percussive sound.
A technique where the pick moves in one continuous motion across multiple strings to play arpeggios.
Using the fingers of the picking hand to hammer-on notes on the fretboard.
Playing a series of notes smoothly and connectedly, often using hammer-ons and pull-offs.
High-pitched, bell-like tones produced by lightly touching the string at specific nodes.
A clamp-like device used to shorten the playable length of strings, effectively changing the key of open chords.
A chord where the index finger acts like a nut, pressing down multiple strings across a single fret.
The default guitar tuning: E2, A2, D3, G3, B3, E4.
Lowering the low E string by one whole step to D.
The number assigned to a note based on its position in a scale (e.g., the 5th degree of C Major is G).
A visual representation of the relationships between the 12 tones of the chromatic scale and their corresponding key signatures.
Seven different scales derived from the major scale, each starting on a different note (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, etc.).
The minor key that shares the same key signature as a specific major key (e.g., A Minor is the relative minor of C Major).
The process of moving a piece of music or a set of chords to a different key.
A visual representation of guitar strings and fret numbers, showing where to place fingers.
The traditional system of writing music using a five-line staff and note symbols.
A grid-based visual showing where to place fingers on the fretboard for a specific chord.
A simplified form of notation containing only the melody, lyrics, and chord symbols.
Symbols like 'p' (piano/soft) or 'f' (forte/loud) that indicate the volume of the music.
